data.alert_description: Investigate network reachability failure: source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 (0/25 pings).

sources.name: get_running_config router1
get_running_config router2
get_running_config router3

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so replies from 172.16.0.12 are not sent back to R1's LAN (they follow R2's default toward R3 and are lost).
evidence: R1 has a specific route for 172.16.0.0/24 pointing at R2: routing -> prefix 172.16.0.0 mask 255.255.255.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.2
R2 routing table shows only a default route: routing -> prefix 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.6 (no route for 192.168.0.0/24 present)
R2 is directly connected to R1 on Serial0/1/0 (10.0.0.2), so absence of a return route on R2 will cause R2 to forward traffic to its default rather than back to 192.168.0.0/24

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so replies from 172.16.0.12 are not sent back to R1's LAN (they follow R2's default toward R3 and are lost).
evidence: R1 has a specific route for 172.16.0.0/24 pointing at R2: routing -> prefix 172.16.0.0 mask 255.255.255.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.2
R2 routing table shows only a default route: routing -> prefix 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.6 (no route for 192.168.0.0/24 present)
R2 is directly connected to R1 on Serial0/1/0 (10.0.0.2), so absence of a return route on R2 will cause R2 to forward traffic to its default rather than back to 192.168.0.0/24

data.intent_description: Investigate network reachability failure: source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 (0/25 pings).

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so replies from 172.16.0.12 are not sent back to R1's LAN (they follow R2's default toward R3 and are lost).
evidence: R1 has a specific route for 172.16.0.0/24 pointing at R2: routing -> prefix 172.16.0.0 mask 255.255.255.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.2
R2 routing table shows only a default route: routing -> prefix 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0 fwd-list fwd: 10.0.0.6 (no route for 192.168.0.0/24 present)
R2 is directly connected to R1 on Serial0/1/0 (10.0.0.2), so absence of a return route on R2 will cause R2 to forward traffic to its default rather than back to 192.168.0.0/24

data.intent_description: Source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 because R2 has no return route for 192.168.0.0/24; after remediation, replies from 172.16.0.12 should route back to 192.168.0.12 and pings should succeed.

plan.problem: R2 lacks a route for 192.168.0.0/24, causing asymmetric routing/blackholing of replies from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1) to restore the return path.

parsed.intent_description: Source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 because R2 has no return route for 192.168.0.0/24; after remediation, replies from 172.16.0.12 should route back to 192.168.0.12 and pings should succeed.

plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1) to restore the return path.

sources.name: get_running_config router1
get_running_config router2
get_running_config router3

evidence: Latest get_running_config for router2 shows routing table contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "mask": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.6" }] — no route for 192.168.0.0/24 present.
R2 is directly connected to R1: R2 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.2/255.255.255.252 (network_db link evidence shows R1 Serial0/1/0 10.0.0.1/30 <-> R2 Serial0/1/0 10.0.0.2/30).

R2 has an access-list named R2_TO_R1 containing a deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443, followed by a permit ip any any (so general connectivity is not fully blocked by the ACL, but HTTPS between these subnets would be denied).
remaining_issues: Router R2 still lacks a return route to 192.168.0.0/24 (no static or connected/OSPF route present). Add static route 192.168.0.0/24 next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1) or ensure dynamic routing carries the prefix.
R2 access-list R2_TO_R1 contains an explicit deny for TCP dst-port 443 from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 — if HTTPS traffic between those networks is required, the ACL must be adjusted.

evidence: Latest get_running_config for router2 shows routing table contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "mask": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.6" }] — no route for 192.168.0.0/24 present.

R2 Serial0/1/0 access-group out acl R2_TO_R1

sources.name: get_running_config router1
get_running_config router2
get_running_config router3

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for 192.168.0.0/24, so replies from 172.16.0.12 have no known next-hop back to 192.168.0.12.
evidence: R2 running-config 'routing' contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0" -> "fwd": "10.0.0.6"; no entry for 192.168.0.0/24 (get_running_config for R2).

R1 has a route for 172.16.0.0 via 10.0.0.2 (R1 routing: "prefix": "172.16.0.0" -> "fwd": "10.0.0.2"), confirming forward path exists from 192.168.0.12 toward 172.16.0.12 but R2 lacks the reciprocal route.

root_causes.cause: A previous attempt to add a static route on R2 failed (400 error). The add_static_route call used next_hop 10.0.0.2 (R2's own address) which is incorrect and likely caused the API error, so the route was not installed.
evidence: previous_changes shows add_static_route on router2 with args: {"network": "192.168.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "next_hop": "10.0.0.2"}.
That request returned an error: {"status": "error","message": "Client error '400 Bad Request' ..."} (previous_changes result).

That request returned an error: {"status": "error","message": "Client error '400 Bad Request' ..."} (previous_changes result).

data.intent_description: Source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 because R2 has no return route for 192.168.0.0/24; after remediation replies from 172.16.0.12 should route back to 192.168.0.12 and pings should succeed.

plan.problem: Router2 has no route for 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 is unroutable.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.1 (assumed R1). Previous add_static_route failed because next_hop was incorrectly set to 10.0.0.2 (R2's own address).

plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.1 (assumed R1). Previous add_static_route failed because next_hop was incorrectly set to 10.0.0.2 (R2's own address).

evidence: get_running_config (router2) shows in routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list an entry: {"prefix": "192.168.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{"fwd": "10.0.0.1"}] } — confirming R2 now has a route for 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1.
Network DB recent parsed config for R2 (call_BGyJnOr477LZQoKtYBAMx0MP) includes the same static route entry for 192.168.0.0/24 -> 10.0.0.1 (last_seen_ts: 2026-05-28T14:30:00Z).
remaining_issues: R2 access-list 'R2_TO_R1' contains an ACE that DENYs tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 — this may block HTTPS traffic between those networks if relevant.

messages.content:
What was observed
- Host 192.168.0.12 could reach 172.16.0.12 one-way because R2 had no return route for 192.168.0.0/24.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- R2 lacked a static route for 192.168.0.0/24, so replies from 172.16.0.12 had no next-hop back to 192.168.0.12.
- A prior remediation attempt failed because the static route was submitted with next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R2’s own address), causing a 400 error.

If changes were made
- Added static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1).

Verification
- Passed: R2 running-config and network DB now show route {prefix: 192.168.0.0, mask: 255.255.255.0, fwd: 10.0.0.1}. Return path is present and pings/traffic should now succeed.

What remains
- R2 ACL "R2_TO_R1" has an ACE denying TCP from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 — this may block HTTPS between the networks. Recommend reviewing/modifying the ACL if HTTPS traffic must be allowed.

data.last_message:
What was observed
- Host 192.168.0.12 could reach 172.16.0.12 one-way because R2 had no return route for 192.168.0.0/24.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- R2 lacked a static route for 192.168.0.0/24, so replies from 172.16.0.12 had no next-hop back to 192.168.0.12.
- A prior remediation attempt failed because the static route was submitted with next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R2’s own address), causing a 400 error.

If changes were made
- Added static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1).

Verification
- Passed: R2 running-config and network DB now show route {prefix: 192.168.0.0, mask: 255.255.255.0, fwd: 10.0.0.1}. Return path is present and pings/traffic should now succeed.

What remains
- R2 ACL "R2_TO_R1" has an ACE denying TCP from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 — this may block HTTPS between the networks. Recommend reviewing/modifying the ACL if HTTPS traffic must be allowed.
